summary: Gruber offers a straightforward explanation for HP's sudden abandonment of Palm/WebOS and its PC business: CEO Léo Apotheker, an enterprise software guy from SAP, was never interested in consumer hardware. Palm was Mark Hurd's acquisition, not Apotheker's, and when Apotheker took over he was already planning to reshape HP into an enterprise consulting company. The TouchPad got a single chance to prove itself and failed, giving Apotheker the excuse he needed. Gruber argues Apotheker's real play was acquiring Autonomy, an enterprise software company, which aligned with his actual vision for HP.
key_points: Palm was acquired under previous CEO Mark Hurd for $1.2 billion, making it an inherited obligation for Apotheker rather than a strategic priority
Apotheker's background as SAP CEO made his intentions predictable: reshape HP into an enterprise consulting company
The TouchPad was given a single-strike opportunity with no patience for building market traction
Apotheker's real strategic move was acquiring Autonomy, announced simultaneously with the consumer hardware exit
money_quotes: You don't bring in an enterprise consulting guy to turn around a PC and device maker. You bring in an enterprise consulting guy to turn a PC and device maker into an enterprise consulting company.
Honestly, we all should have seen this coming.
Palm wasn't Apotheker's acquisition. It was Hurd's. And the PC business wasn't why Apotheker took the job.
Apotheker simply never had any interest in the consumer market or product development.
If he'd been named HP's CEO six months earlier, they never would have acquired Palm in the first place.
key_insight: A CEO's background predicts their strategy: hiring an enterprise software executive to run a hardware company meant HP was always going to pivot away from consumer products.
